title: Today's Tech Brief: Gemini’s new AI agent is about as good as Google’s demo
summary: A look at Google's Gemini AI agent and a startup's robot troubles
imageAlt: Image from theverge.com for Gemini’s new AI agent is about as good as Google’s demo
imageCaption: Gemini’s new AI agent is about as good as Google’s demo (theverge.com)

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to Today's Tech Brief. I've got two tech stories for you today that caught my eye. First up, Google's new AI agent, Gemini Spark, is making waves. I'm looking at reports from The Verge, and it seems this agent is impressive, but not quite living up to the hype... yet. It's like that friend who's really cool, but you're not sure if they're worth the effort... yet.

Gemini Spark is Google's latest foray into AI. In simple terms, it's a platform that uses artificial intelligence to perform tasks, kind of like a super-smart virtual assistant. The Verge got hands-on with it, and their take is that it's impressive, but the cost might not be worth it... for now. I mean, who doesn't love a good AI demo, right? It's like watching a magic show, but instead of magic, it's code and computers.

Next, a San Francisco robotics startup is in legal trouble for allegedly trashing Airbnb rentals to test their robots. I'm talking robot vacuum cleaners here, not robot overlords... yet. According to Ars Technica, an Airbnb host is taking them to court, and it's a bit of a mess. The startup was testing their robots in real-world settings, but it seems they didn't quite get the necessary permissions. I guess that's what happens when you try to shortcut the 'permission' part of 'testing in real-world settings'.

My quick read: today's tech stories are less about one giant thunderclap and more about a lively weather pattern. A little platform drama here, a little artificial intelligence irony there, and suddenly the industry has enough material for a very caffeinated morning show.
